entry_analysis.summary: The structural reading treats Villa Noailles as a stepped system of cubic blocks, terraces and retaining relations rather than as a single pure object.
The tectonic reading separates block volumes, terraces, roof planes, circulation paths, garden geometry and light surfaces.
Spatial order is episodic and kinetic: the villa is experienced through rooms, terraces, stairs, views and garden fragments rather than one continuous free plan.
The Guevrekian garden and hillside terraces turn the house into a constructed landscape of leisure, abstraction and viewpoint.
Villa Noailles mutates the modern villa into an artist-house and leisure machine, less doctrinal than Villa Savoye and more programmatically dispersed.
Suggested filters: modern villa, avant-garde domesticity, movement sequence, terrace system, cubist garden, leisure, artist house, early modernism.
The structural reading foregrounds a steel-frame order that frees the living level from conventional room-bearing walls and allows material screens to organize space.
The tectonic reading separates frame, glass, curtain, Onyx wall, timber wall and floor plane into layered elements that produce fluid domestic space.
Spatial order is defined by zones rather than closed rooms: living, dining, music and view fields flow around material partitions and curtains.
Onyx, glass, steel, textile and timber are not decoration only; they act as atmospheric and spatial instruments within the free plan.
Haus Tugendhat mutates the villa into a spatial instrument for modern living, where representational domestic rooms dissolve into linked fields and material thresholds.
Suggested filters: modern villa, Mies, free plan, curtain screen, material wall, steel frame, Brno, early modernism, domestic modernity.
The house is read as a reinforced-concrete frame that separates load-bearing order from facade and plan, making pilotis, slab and free enclosure the structural grammar of the project.
The tectonic reading separates the lifted frame, white envelope, horizontal window band, ramp, service cores and roof-garden plane as distinct modern layers.
Spatial order is choreographed as a promenade: arrival below the raised volume, ascent by ramp, movement through living spaces and release onto the roof terrace.
The material reading foregrounds white rendered surfaces, reinforced concrete, glass bands and roof-garden ground as an abstract modern envelope.
Villa Savoye operates as a manifesto-house: a domestic program used to demonstrate a general architectural system rather than only a private residence.
Suggested filters: modern villa, manifesto house, five points, promenade architecturale, pilotis, free plan, reinforced-concrete frame, Le Corbusier, early modernism.
The circulation layer is defined by the car arrival trace and the ramp promenade from ground level to roof garden.
Diagrammatic vector plans and section have been reconstructed as rights-safe study layers for analysis and future procedural 3D modeling, not as measured construction drawings.

Six pavilion roofs act as canopy-like structural fields. Vertical and lateral stability is described through load-bearing window frames and T/L-shaped service cores, with a visible concrete slab tying the system together.

The tectonic reading separates terrain cut, concrete ground datum, pavilion roofs, glass enclosure, and service cores. The house is understood as a landscape-embedded canopy cluster rather than a single closed volume.
The spatial order is radial and centripetal: six interlocking pavilions form petal-like rooms around a central open courtyard, distributing public and private zones through degrees of openness.
Glass, concrete and earth/topography form the primary material system: transparent pavilion edges meet a heavy ground datum and hillside thermal envelope.
Movement is organized around the central court and between pavilion wings, with landscape-facing rooms opening outward and more private zones embedded into the slope.
The project extends the Glass House lineage by breaking the single transparent box into a flower-like cluster of pavilions, making landscape and privacy part of the typological revision.

Suggested filters: Afasia source, private residence, glass-house typology, landscape integration, hillside, courtyard, canopy structure, core-stabilized tectonics, contemporary reuse/sustainability sector.

entry_relations.description: The structural prototype becomes an architectural language of pilotis, free plan, and free facade.
Institutional and domestic modernism share glass, openness, and a new relationship between life and technical systems.
Le Corbusier's house logic and urban doctrine share a belief in order, circulation, and functional separation.
The isolated manifesto house and the ideal city both translate Le Corbusier's ordering systems into spatial diagrams.
Both houses test modern living through open plans, technical refinement, and a new relationship between domestic space and abstraction.
The five-points house later scales toward collective housing, roof programs, pilotis and standardized modern life.
Modern domesticity becomes a field of movement, curtains, views, and flexible rooms.
Both villas turn domestic life into modern spatial experimentation, but Noailles emphasizes movement and leisure while Savoye crystallizes a manifesto.
Domestic avant-garde culture and institutional modern pedagogy share a belief in modern life as designed environment.
The private leisure villa and collective housing prototype expose different social programs within early modern domestic experimentation.
The free-plan villa and the collective block both test modern living through services, open spatial fields, and technical systems.
Mies's domestic fluidity sits in contrast to nineteenth-century urban grid rationalization, showing different scales of modern order.
Both works connect modern material systems, glass, technical precision, and new ways of organizing life.
